title: God as Father
intro: Of all the names God could have given us for Himself, He chose Father. Not merely Creator, not Judge, not King — though He is all of these. He chose the most intimate and relational name available, and He made it the one Jesus taught us to use when we pray.
reading:
God has always related to His people as a Father. Deuteronomy 32:6 asks Israel directly: is He not your Father, who created you, who made you and established you? Isaiah 63:16 cries out to Him as Redeemer and Father. Jeremiah 31:9 records God Himself saying: I am a father to Israel.

But the New Testament opens something new. Through Christ, we are not merely creatures of a creating Father — we are adopted children. Matthew 6:9 places Father as the very first word of the prayer Jesus taught His disciples. John 1:12-13 says that to all who received Him, He gave the right to become children of God — born not of blood or the will of man, but of God.

Romans 8:15-16 reveals the depth of this adoption: we have received the Spirit of adoption as sons, by whom we cry Abba — Father. The Spirit Himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God. Abba is not a formal title. It is the word a child uses. It carries closeness, safety, and the freedom to come without ceremony.

1 John 3:1 steps back in wonder at the very fact of it: see what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God — and so we are.

You are not an orphan trying to earn your way into His house. You are a child. You have been brought in.
reflection: How does your relationship with your earthly father — for better or worse — shape the way you approach God as Father? What would it mean to let Scripture redefine that image?
abide: Abba, Father — I come not as a stranger but as a child. Let me live from that place today. Not striving to earn Your love, but resting in the love that already called me Your own.
